title: Napoleon's 1812 Russian Campaign
description: The Grande Armée's advance to Moscow and its catastrophic winter retreat, June–December 1812. Strength and temperature follow Minard's 1869 chart. Interim content — density and sourcing deepen in a later research pass.

vertices.label: Back onto the Smolensk road at Mozhaisk

moments.title: Crossing the Niemen
moments.body: The Grande Armée — some 450,000 men in all — begins pouring across the Niemen into Russian territory. No enemy stands to oppose it.

moments.title: The Russians will not stand
moments.body: Barclay de Tolly withdraws rather than give battle, drawing the invaders deeper into a scorched countryside.

moments.title: Vilna without a fight
moments.body: Napoleon enters Vilna. Already the roads behind him are littered with broken-down wagons and dead horses.

moments.title: Heat, dust, and dying horses
moments.body: Searing heat and thunderstorms wreck the supply train. Thousands of horses die in days; men fall out by the roadside.

moments.title: Disease thins the ranks
moments.body: Dysentery and typhus spread through the columns. The army is bleeding strength before it has fought a single battle.

moments.title: Vitebsk — the quarry slips away
moments.body: Napoleon reaches Vitebsk hoping for a decisive battle, but the Russians are gone again. He briefly considers halting here for the winter.

moments.title: The decision to press on
moments.body: Against the advice of his marshals, Napoleon resolves to march on Smolensk and force the battle he needs.

moments.title: Smolensk in flames
moments.body: Two days of fighting leave the ancient city burning. The Russians slip away eastward under cover of the smoke.

moments.title: A hollow victory
moments.body: The French hold Smolensk's ruins but have again failed to trap the Russian army. The road east is open — and devastated.

moments.title: The Shevardino redoubt
moments.body: A vicious preliminary fight for an earthwork southwest of Borodino signals that the Russians mean, at last, to stand.

moments.title: The Battle of Borodino begins
moments.body: At dawn 250,000 men and over 1,000 guns collide on a front a few miles wide. It will be the bloodiest single day of the age.

moments.title: The Great Redoubt falls
moments.body: After hours of slaughter the Raevsky Redoubt is finally stormed, but the Russian line bends without breaking.

moments.title: A field of the dead
moments.body: Around 70,000 men are dead or wounded. The Russians withdraw in good order; Napoleon has his 'victory' but not his decisive result.

moments.title: Moscow, but no peace
moments.body: The Grande Armée enters a Moscow that is silent and three-quarters empty. No delegation comes to offer terms.

moments.title: Moscow burns
moments.body: Fires break out across the city and spread through the night, consuming the shelter and supplies the army had counted on.

moments.title: Waiting in the ashes
moments.body: Napoleon lingers among the ruins, sending peace feelers to a Tsar who will not answer, while the season turns against him.

moments.title: First snow
moments.body: Light snow flurries fall on Moscow — a warning of the winter that everyone but the Emperor seems to feel coming.

moments.title: The retreat begins
moments.body: With no peace and no supplies, Napoleon abandons Moscow and marches southwest, hoping for an untouched road home.

moments.title: Turned back at Maloyaroslavets
moments.body: Kutuzov blocks the southern road in a savage fight. The Grande Armée is forced back onto the ruined Smolensk highway.

moments.title: The road of bones
moments.body: The army retraces the devastated route past the unburied dead of Borodino. Starvation and cold begin in earnest.

moments.title: Rearguard mauled at Vyazma
moments.body: Russian forces fall on the strung-out columns; discipline in several corps begins to dissolve.

moments.title: The first hard frost
moments.body: The temperature plunges. Ill-shod men freeze where they fall; horses die and are butchered on the spot.

moments.title: Smolensk offers no salvation
moments.body: The magazines at Smolensk are found nearly bare and are quickly plundered. There is no rest and little food here.

moments.title: The army disintegrates
moments.body: Leaving Smolensk in scattered groups, the Grande Armée is becoming a mob of stragglers around a shrinking core of fighting men.

moments.title: Cut to pieces at Krasny
moments.body: Ney's rearguard is nearly annihilated and fights its way out across the ice. Thousands are killed or captured.

moments.title: Burning the eagles at Orsha
moments.body: Cannon are spiked and standards burned to keep them from Russian hands. What remains crosses the Dnieper.

moments.title: The bridges are lost
moments.body: The Russians seize the crossings at Borisov. For a moment the whole army seems trapped against the Berezina.

moments.title: Crossing the Berezina
moments.body: Engineers build two trestle bridges at Studenka, standing in the freezing river. The remnant army begins to cross.

moments.title: Carnage at the bridges
moments.body: Russian guns rake both banks. Amid panic among the stragglers, thousands drown or are trampled at the crossings.

moments.title: The bridges are burned
moments.body: To stop the pursuit, the bridges are fired with thousands still on the east bank. Their cries carry across the river.

moments.title: The Emperor departs
moments.body: At Smorgon, Napoleon hands over command and races ahead by sleigh to Paris to raise a new army.

moments.title: The coldest night
moments.body: The thermometer falls toward −37°C. Men freeze around dying campfires; whole units simply cease to exist by morning.

moments.title: A ghost army reaches Vilna
moments.body: A few thousand skeletal survivors stumble into Vilna, only to be driven on again as the Russians close in.

moments.title: Back over the Niemen
moments.body: Of the host that crossed in June, perhaps ten thousand recross as a formed body. The campaign is over; the myth of invincibility is broken.

narrativeBeats.text: Dawn: the French grand battery opens fire across the Kolocha.
The Bagration flèches change hands again and again in the smoke.
The Raevsky Redoubt — the Great Redoubt — is stormed at last.
Nightfall: the Russians hold their ground, then withdraw. The field is a charnel house.
The army enters a silent, half-abandoned city.
Fires break out and race through the wooden streets.
Three-quarters of Moscow lies in ashes.
A last peace overture is sent to the Tsar. No answer comes.
Pontooniers build two trestle bridges at Studenka in the freezing current.
The Guard and remaining fighting units cross to the west bank.
Russian batteries open on both banks; panic seizes the mass of stragglers.
The bridges are burned with thousands still stranded on the east bank.

conditions.label: The Berezina and the final collapse

attribution: Army strength and retreat temperatures after C. J. Minard, Carte figurative (1869) — public domain.
sources: Charles Minard, Carte figurative des pertes successives en hommes de l'Armée Française dans la campagne de Russie 1812–1813 (1869).

Standard campaign chronology (e.g. Chandler, The Campaigns of Napoleon; Zamoyski, 1812: Napoleon's Fatal March on Moscow). Figures are interim approximations pending a dedicated content-research pass.
